epigraph.text:
Honor the Lord with your wealth, with the firstfruits 
of all your crops.

content.text:
The word *percent* means "per hundred." When we say 25 percent, 
we mean 25 out of every 100. This simple idea provides a universal 
language for comparing parts of different wholes.

content.title: Percent
content.content:
A **percent** is a ratio that compares a number to 100. The 
symbol \% means "per hundred" or "out of 100."
\[
 n\% = \frac{n}{100}
\]

content.title: Interpreting percents
content.problem:
Interpret each percent as a ratio.

1. 50\% means 50 out of 100

2. 7\% means 7 out of 100

3. 100\% means 100 out of 100 (the whole thing)

4. 150\% means 150 out of 100 (more than the whole)

content.title: Visualizing percents
content.problem:
A 10 $\times$ 10 grid has 100 small squares. If 35 squares are 
shaded, what percent is shaded?
content.solution:
Since 35 out of 100 squares are shaded:
\[
 \text{Percent shaded} = 35\%
\]

content.text:
Percents appear everywhere in modern life. Here are some common 
examples:

content.title: Real-world percents
content.problem:
A nutrition label states that one serving provides 20\% of the 
recommended daily value of iron. What does this mean?
content.solution:
This means one serving provides 20 out of every 100 parts of the 
iron you need daily. You would need 5 servings (if this were your 
only iron source) to reach 100\% of your daily iron needs.

content.text:
Certain percent-fraction-decimal equivalents appear so often that 
you should memorize them:

content.title: Using common percents
content.problem:
Use the table above to find each value mentally.

1. 50\% of 80

2. 25\% of 40

3. 10\% of 250
content.solution:
1. 50\% is half, so 50\% of 80 = 40

2. 25\% is one-fourth, so 25\% of 40 = 10

3. 10\% is one-tenth, so 10\% of 250 = 25

content.text:
A percent can be greater than 100\%. This indicates an amount 
larger than the whole to which we're comparing.

content.title: Percents over 100\%
content.problem:
A company's sales increased from \$50{,}000 last year to 
\$75{,}000 this year. Express this year's sales as a percent 
of last year's.
content.solution:
\[
 \frac{\$75{,}000}{\$50{,}000} = \frac{75}{50} = 1.5 = 150\%
\]
This year's sales are 150\% of last year's sales (a 50\% increase).

content.content:
Be careful with language: "150\% of" something means 1.5 times 
that amount, while "a 50\% increase" means adding 50\% more to 
the original.

content.text:
Very small portions are expressed as percents less than 1\%. These 
appear in contexts like interest rates, scientific measurements, 
and statistics.

content.title: Small percents
content.problem:
Express each as a percent.

1. 0.5 out of 100 = 0.5\%

2. 0.05 out of 100 = 0.05\%

3. $\frac{1}{200}$ (half of one percent) = 0.5\%

content.title: The Tithe Across Scripture
content.content:
The tithe (10\%) appears throughout the Bible. Abraham gave a 
tenth to Melchizedek (Genesis 14:20). The Mosaic law established 
tithing as a regular practice (Leviticus 27:30). Jesus affirmed 
tithing while emphasizing "justice, mercy, and faithfulness" 
(Matthew 23:23). Understanding percents helps us calculate tithes 
and think mathematically about stewardship.

marginNotes.text:
A tithe of everything from the 
land, whether grain from the soil or fruit from the trees, belongs 
to the Lord; it is holy to the Lord.

Isaac planted crops in that land 
and the same year reaped a hundredfold, because the Lord blessed 
him.

marginNotes.author: John Allen Paulos
marginNotes.text:
Innumeracy—an inability to deal 
comfortably with the fundamental notions of number and chance—plagues 
far too many otherwise knowledgeable citizens.
